text: The surgical team has entered the patient's abdomen through the main umbilical port, initiating the laparoscopic cholestectomy procedure.

The surgeon observes fibrinous adhesions around the gallbladder, indicating possible mild perforation. The surrounding biliary structures appear normal, and the rest of the abdominal surface is visually intact.
The surgeon inspects the abdominal cavity, noting the presence of gas in the stomach. An incision is made in the epigastric region of the skin to introduce an additional surgical port.

The surgeon positions the camera close to a mass located on the right side in the mid-axillary line and anterior axillary line. Care is taken to avoid injuring nearby structures such as the colon and small intestine while establishing the fourth port.

The surgeon carefully introduces an instrument under the region, observing the tip as it is established. The focus shifts to removing flimsy adhesions around the gallbladder, assessing their strength and fibrous nature to facilitate the gallbladder's removal.
The surgeon attempts to identify the gallbladder fundus, which is not clearly visible, and prepares to perform dissection to confirm its location amidst challenging adhesions.

The surgeon achieves progress in clearing the omentum from the extended gallbladder, focusing on the fundus. Gentle manipulation is used to separate the tissue planes, with the option to cut or cauterize under vision if necessary.

The surgeon carefully identifies the correct anatomical planes, revealing the globular gallbladder as it is gradually exposed. Adhesions are present between the gallbladder and the liver, which are gently pulled apart. The surgeon remains cautious of nearby structures such as the colon and duodenum during the dissection.
The surgeon encounters a gallbladder full of pus and prepares to aspirate the contents using suction, ensuring cleanliness and careful handling to avoid complications.

The surgeon observes the gallbladder wall, which appears gangrenous with pus points and is very slippery. The gallbladder is filled with infected bile and mucus, which are being aspirated out under general anesthesia.

The surgeon uses a suction tip to gently push the omentum, revealing the gallbladder as it slowly comes into view. The suction tip is preferred for its round shape, causing less trauma during dissection.
The surgeon uses a fundal grasper to manipulate the gallbladder, which appears rotten and full of pus. The gallbladder is gently pushed back to improve visibility and access.

The surgeon reaches the base of the gallbladder, identifying its attachments to the common bile duct and common hepatic duct. The blood supply is delineated, and the cystic artery is clipped to secure the gallbladder's blood supply. The peritoneal coverage is being carefully dissected.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to gently peel away the peritoneal coverings from the gallbladder, revealing its attachments. The tissue appears rotten and fragile, requiring delicate manipulation to avoid damage.
The surgeon uses a Maryland curved dissector and an infundibulum holder to manipulate the heart mass pouch and infundibulum, gently dissecting on the right side, left side, and below the gallbladder.
The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder from the liver bed, identifying the gallbladder and ducts while removing decomposed tissue that makes the area slippery.
The surgeon carefully separates the gallbladder from the liver, creating a posterior window to access the Calot's triangle. The triangle gradually opens, revealing the cystic duct and cystic artery.

The surgeon has opened part of the Calot's triangle, revealing fascia due to infection. The cystic duct and cystic artery are identified, but further dissection is required to achieve the critical view of safety, ensuring clear visualization of the cystic duct, cystic artery, common hepatic duct, and liver tissue. The gallbladder is mobilized, and the surgeon carefully dissects from above, moving downwards, using an instrument to clarify the structures.

The surgeon gently dissects around the lymph node capsule, which appears soft, to ensure the critical view of safety is clear. The dissection reveals the biliary ducts, including the right and left hepatic ducts, which join to form the common bile duct. The surgeon identifies fascia and a possible artery in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs suction in the area, revealing the swollen and rotten gallbladder with thickened peritoneum. Visible structures include a vein, an artery, and the common bile duct and common hepatic duct. The lower portion of the fascia is seen over the common hepatic duct, which remains unharmed as the anatomy is clearly visualized.

The surgeon applies clips to the cystic duct using a clip applicator, ensuring the tip is visible on the opposite side. The critical view of safety is confirmed, showing the common bile duct, cystic duct, cystic artery, and surrounding fossa.
